title: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?
rawPassageText:
READING PASSAGE 3
You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27-40, which are based on Reading Passage 3 below.

What Makes a Good Archive?

In a library basement, a paper box contains a city official's diary, several theatre tickets, a cracked photograph frame and a recipe written on the back of an electricity bill. At first glance, the diary seems to be the important object. It has dates, names and references to public events. The recipe looks accidental, perhaps even trivial. Yet years later, a historian studying domestic life may find the recipe more revealing than the diary. It may show what ingredients were available, what language was used at home, and how ordinary people adapted public shortages to private routines. An archive is full of such reversals.

Many people imagine archives as neutral storehouses: places where the past is simply kept. But no archive can keep everything. Someone must decide what is collected, how it is described, where it is placed and who may see it. Even the most careful archive is therefore an argument about what might matter in the future. This does not make archives dishonest. It makes them human. Their value depends not only on preservation but also on the awareness that preservation involves choice.

The problem has become more complicated in the digital age. Earlier archivists often struggled with scarcity: too few documents survived, or those that did survive were damaged, private or scattered. Today, many institutions face the opposite difficulty. Emails, photographs, recordings, messages and drafts can be produced in enormous quantities. Keeping everything may sound democratic, but abundance can bury meaning as effectively as absence. A million unlabelled files are not necessarily more useful than a single carefully described box.

Archivists therefore spend much of their time on context. A photograph without a date, place or subject may be visually interesting but historically weak. A letter without information about its writer and recipient may be difficult to interpret. A digital file without metadata can become what some curators call an "orphaned object": technically present, but separated from the conditions that would make it understandable. In this sense, archiving is not the same as storage. Storage keeps material. Archiving prepares material to be found, questioned and connected.

Different specialists define the work in different ways. Alina Markov, who manages a legal archive, says that an archive is "a promise that evidence can be checked". For her, the essential issue is verification: future readers must be able to trace a claim back to a document. James Ordu, a community archivist, disagrees with the image of archives as places that merely collect. He argues that materials should return knowledge to the communities from which they came, especially when those communities have been studied by outsiders. Nina Bell, a digital curator, insists that metadata can be as important as the object itself; without context, she says, a file may survive while its meaning disappears. Soren Lykke, a paper conservator, warns that preservation can create an illusion of permanence. Some marks of decay, repair or handling reveal how an object lived, and a perfect copy may lose that evidence. Priya Shah, an oral historian, is most interested in silence: whose voices were never recorded, whose letters were never saved, and whose absence tells us something about power.

Each definition reflects a professional concern. The legal archivist emphasises proof, the community archivist responsibility, the digital curator context, the conservator material history, and the oral historian absence. But these views share a common assumption: an archive is not a pile of old things. It is a structure built for future interpretation.

I should declare my own bias. As a historian, I am drawn to the records of ordinary people: shopping lists, school notebooks, marginal comments, repair receipts and family photographs with names written on the back. Such materials rarely announce their importance. They become important when a new question makes them visible. The age of digital abundance has not removed the need for archives; it has changed the difficulty. The central challenge is no longer only preservation, but interpretation. We do not merely need more traces of the past. We need better ways of asking what those traces can tell us.

Questions 27-32
Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.
Write the correct letter in boxes 27-32 on your answer sheet.

27 The writer mentions the recipe written on an electricity bill in order to show that
A official documents are usually less accurate than personal ones.
B apparently minor objects can become historically significant.
C recipes are the most reliable source for studying domestic life.
D archives should avoid collecting public records.

28 What is the writer's view of archives in the second paragraph?
A They are neutral places where the past is preserved without judgment.
B They are valuable only when they contain a very large quantity of material.
C They involve choices about what may matter in the future.
D They are usually dishonest because they exclude some materials.

29 In the third paragraph, the writer suggests that digital abundance
A has solved the main problems faced by archivists.
B can make useful meaning harder to find.
C has made physical archives unnecessary.
D is less democratic than older forms of record keeping.

30 What does the phrase "orphaned object" refer to?
A a file that has been deliberately deleted
B a document that is too damaged to preserve
C an item separated from the context needed to understand it
D an object that belongs to a private family collection

31 What is the main purpose of the fifth paragraph?
A to compare several definitions of archival work
B to argue that legal archives are the most important kind
C to show that digital records are more reliable than paper ones
D to explain why oral history is replacing written documents

32 In the final paragraph, the writer says that he is especially interested in
A records that reveal ordinary lives when new questions are asked.
B official papers produced by governments and courts.
C archives that contain only complete and undamaged objects.
D digital files that can be stored in very large numbers.

Questions 33-37
Look at the following statements (Questions 33-37) and the list of specialists below.
Match each statement with the correct specialist, A-E.
Write the correct letter, A-E, in boxes 33-37 on your answer sheet.
NB You may use any letter more than once.

33 This specialist emphasises returning knowledge to the people connected with the records.
34 This specialist believes that contextual information can be as important as the item preserved.
35 This specialist defines archives mainly in relation to checking evidence.
36 This specialist focuses on voices and materials that were never recorded.
37 This specialist warns that preservation may create a false impression that nothing has changed.

List of Specialists
A Alina Markov
B James Ordu
C Nina Bell
D Soren Lykke
E Priya Shah

Questions 38-40
Complete the summary below.
Choose NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS from the passage for each answer.
Write your answers in boxes 38-40 on your answer sheet.

The writer's own bias

The writer says that, as a historian, he is attracted to records of 38 ____________, such as shopping lists and school notebooks. He argues that the age of 39 ____________ has changed the work of archives. In his view, the central challenge is now not only preservation, but 40 ____________.
rawAnswerKey:
Question 27: The writer mentions the recipe written on an electricity bill in order to show that

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: Yet years later, a historian studying domestic life may find the recipe more revealing than the diary. It may show what ingredients were available, what language was used at home, and how ordinary people adapted public shortages to private routines. An archive is full of such reversals.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะ recipe ที่ดู trivial อาจ more revealing than diary และ archive is full of such reversals ตรง minor objects can become historically significant ตัวเลือก A ผิดเพราะไม่ได้เปรียบความแม่นยำ official vs personal ตัวเลือก C ผิดเพราะไม่ได้บอกว่า recipes เป็นแหล่งที่เชื่อถือที่สุด ตัวเลือก D ผิดเพราะไม่ได้แนะนำให้เลี่ยง public records

Paraphrased Vocabulary: minor objects historically significant = recipe more revealing than diary / such reversals; answer signal = B



Question 28: What is the writer's view of archives in the second paragraph?

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: no archive can keep everything. Someone must decide what is collected, how it is described, where it is placed and who may see it. Even the most careful archive is therefore an argument about what might matter in the future.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C เพราะ no archive can keep everything และ archive is an argument about what might matter in the future ตรง involve choices ตัวเลือก A ผิดเพราะ passage ปฏิเสธ neutral storehouses ตัวเลือก B ผิดเพราะ abundance ไม่ได้ทำให้มีค่ามากขึ้นเสมอ ตัวเลือก D ผิดเพราะบอก This does not make archives dishonest

Paraphrased Vocabulary: choices about future = argument about what might matter; involve choices = someone must decide what is collected; answer signal = C



Question 29: In the third paragraph, the writer suggests that digital abundance

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: abundance can bury meaning as effectively as absence. A million unlabelled files are not necessarily more useful than a single carefully described box.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะ abundance can bury meaning และ million unlabelled files ไม่จำเป็นต้อง useful กว่า carefully described box ตัวเลือก A ผิดเพราะ digital age ทำปัญหาซับซ้อนขึ้น ไม่ได้แก้ปัญหา ตัวเลือก C ผิดเพราะไม่ได้บอก physical archives ไม่จำเป็น ตัวเลือก D ผิดเพราะ keeping everything may sound democratic

Paraphrased Vocabulary: useful meaning harder to find = bury meaning; digital abundance = enormous quantities / unlabelled files; answer signal = B



Question 30: What does the phrase "orphaned object" refer to?

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: A digital file without metadata can become what some curators call an "orphaned object": technically present, but separated from the conditions that would make it understandable.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C เพราะ orphaned object คือ technically present but separated from conditions that would make it understandable ตัวเลือก A ผิดเพราะไม่ได้บอก deliberately deleted ตัวเลือก B ผิดเพราะไม่ได้พูด too damaged ตัวเลือก D ผิดเพราะเป็น digital file without metadata ไม่ใช่ family collection

Paraphrased Vocabulary: separated from context = separated from conditions that would make it understandable; orphaned object = digital file without metadata; answer signal = C



Question 31: What is the main purpose of the fifth paragraph?

Correct Answer: A

Accepted Answers: A

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: Different specialists define the work in different ways. Alina Markov... James Ordu... Nina Bell... Soren Lykke... Priya Shah...

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ A เพราะย่อหน้าห้าให้ Different specialists define the work in different ways ผ่าน Markov, Ordu, Bell, Lykke, Shah เพื่อ compare definitions ตัวเลือก B ผิดเพราะไม่ได้อ้าง legal archives สำคัญที่สุด ตัวเลือก C ผิดเพราะไม่ได้เปรียบ digital กับ paper ว่า reliable กว่า ตัวเลือก D ผิดเพราะ oral history ไม่ได้แทนที่ written documents

Paraphrased Vocabulary: compare definitions = Different specialists define the work in different ways; answer signal = A



Question 32: In the final paragraph, the writer says that he is especially interested in

Correct Answer: A

Accepted Answers: A

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: I am drawn to the records of ordinary people: shopping lists, school notebooks, marginal comments, repair receipts and family photographs with names written on the back. Such materials rarely announce their importance. They become important when a new question makes them visible.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ A เพราะ writer drawn to records of ordinary people ที่ become important when a new question makes them visible ตัวเลือก B ผิดเพราะสนใจ ordinary ไม่ใช่ official papers ตัวเลือก C ผิดเพราะ Lykke พูดถึง marks of decay ไม่ใช่ complete undamaged only ตัวเลือก D ผิดเพราะ digital abundance เปลี่ยนความยาก ไม่ใช่สนใจจำนวนไฟล์มาก

Paraphrased Vocabulary: ordinary lives + new questions = records of ordinary people / new question makes them visible; answer signal = A



Question 33: This specialist emphasises returning knowledge to the people connected with the records.

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: James Ordu, a community archivist, disagrees with the image of archives as places that merely collect. He argues that materials should return knowledge to the communities from which they came

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะ James Ordu ว่า materials should return knowledge to the communities from which they came ไม่ใช่ Markov (verification) หรือ Bell (metadata)

Paraphrased Vocabulary: returning knowledge = return knowledge to the communities; specialist = James Ordu; answer signal = B



Question 34: This specialist believes that contextual information can be as important as the item preserved.

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: Nina Bell, a digital curator, insists that metadata can be as important as the object itself; without context, she says, a file may survive while its meaning disappears.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C เพราะ Nina Bell บอก metadata can be as important as the object itself ไม่ใช่ Ordu (communities) หรือ Markov (evidence checked)

Paraphrased Vocabulary: contextual information = metadata / context; as important as item = as important as the object itself; specialist = Nina Bell



Question 35: This specialist defines archives mainly in relation to checking evidence.

Correct Answer: A

Accepted Answers: A

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: Alina Markov, who manages a legal archive, says that an archive is "a promise that evidence can be checked". For her, the essential issue is verification

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ A เพราะ Alina Markov ว่า archive is a promise that evidence can be checked และ essential issue is verification ไม่ใช่ Shah (silence) หรือ Lykke (permanence illusion)

Paraphrased Vocabulary: checking evidence = evidence can be checked / verification; specialist = Alina Markov; answer signal = A



Question 36: This specialist focuses on voices and materials that were never recorded.

Correct Answer: E

Accepted Answers: E

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: Priya Shah, an oral historian, is most interested in silence: whose voices were never recorded, whose letters were never saved, and whose absence tells us something about power.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ E เพราะ Priya Shah สนใจ silence: voices never recorded, letters never saved ไม่ใช่ Ordu (return knowledge) หรือ Bell (metadata)

Paraphrased Vocabulary: never recorded = voices were never recorded; specialist = Priya Shah; answer signal = E



Question 37: This specialist warns that preservation may create a false impression that nothing has changed.

Correct Answer: D

Accepted Answers: D

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: Soren Lykke, a paper conservator, warns that preservation can create an illusion of permanence.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ D เพราะ Soren Lykke warns preservation can create an illusion of permanence ซึ่ง paraphrase false impression nothing has changed ไม่ใช่ Bell (meaning disappears) ที่เน้นคนละมุม

Paraphrased Vocabulary: false impression nothing changed = illusion of permanence; specialist = Soren Lykke; answer signal = D



Question 38: he is attracted to records of .................... .

Correct Answer: ordinary people

Accepted Answers: ordinary people

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: I am drawn to the records of ordinary people: shopping lists, school notebooks, marginal comments, repair receipts and family photographs

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ ordinary people เพราะ I am drawn to the records of ordinary people ต้องตอบสองคำจาก passage (NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS) ไม่ใช้ shopping lists หรือ historian

Paraphrased Vocabulary: records of ___ = records of ordinary people; TWO WORDS = ordinary people



Question 39: the age of .................... has changed the work of archives.

Correct Answer: digital abundance

Accepted Answers: digital abundance

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: The age of digital abundance has not removed the need for archives; it has changed the difficulty.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ digital abundance เพราะ The age of digital abundance has changed the difficulty ต้องตอบสองคำตรง phrase ใน passage ไม่ใช้ digital age หรือ enormous quantities

Paraphrased Vocabulary: age of ___ = age of digital abundance; TWO WORDS = digital abundance



Question 40: the central challenge is now not only preservation, but .................... .

Correct Answer: interpretation

Accepted Answers: interpretation

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading March 2026 Passage 3 - What Makes a Good Archive?

Exact Portion: The central challenge is no longer only preservation, but interpretation.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ interpretation เพราะ central challenge is no longer only preservation, but interpretation ต้องคำเดียวจาก passage ไม่ใช้ preservation ซ้ำหรือ difficulty

Paraphrased Vocabulary: not only preservation but = no longer only preservation, but interpretation; ONE WORD = interpretation
